data.title: 2025 was a tough year for Hong Kong’s bar industry, so how are new concepts carving a path forward?
data.description:
Despite this being the year Hong Kong hosted its first edition of World’s 50 Best Bars (with Asia’s 50 Best Bars held in nearby Macau), the scene as a whole suffers from headwinds stemming from declining interest in alcohol and dining out, combined with a significant price gap with the Greater Bay Area, to which tourists and weekend revellers are flocking, as the Post reported earlier this month.
Bars and restaurants close every year, but 2025 saw the closure of extremely recognisable venues that have been serving the city for anywhere between half of to beyond a decade. Foxglove, which celebrated a decade this year, closed its doors last week. The Daily Tot shuttered just a few weeks before that, preceded by Stockton (est. 2014) earlier in the year. Last year, Dr Fern’s Gin Parlour and Please Don’t Tell were closed to make way for major renovations at The Landmark, Mandarin Oriental. The Envoy, The Pottinger’s outdoor terrace bar, and its neighbouring speakeasy Room 309 also served their last drinks in 2024.

“I feel very sad that these bars closed down for good,” says Jo Lo, co-founder of Bar Mind, a minimalist cocktail bar in SoHo. “These are the bars [that] really opened my eyes to Asian and Hong Kong bar culture.” Before opening Bar Mind this year, Lo spent almost six years at Room 309 and The Envoy as part of Tastings Group. In 2022, she won Diageo World Class Hong Kong & Macau Bartender of the Year.
“This year feels like a market correction,” says Ming Cheung, co-founder of 001. “Rents have readjusted in some areas, but not enough. Consumer spending is more cautious. It’s a perfect storm – pre-pandemic business models are colliding with post-pandemic economics and consumer behaviour. It’s a cleansing, but a painful one for the culture.” Originally founded near Gage Street Market in 2010, 001 closed in 2022 before finding a new home a year later in Tai Kwun.

Opening a successful concept in Hong Kong requires a delicate confluence of factors. Rent and operational costs have to be kept as lean as possible. Lo observes that breaking even can take anywhere from six months to two years even with significant initial investment, cost control and effective marketing. John Nugent, founder of The Diplomat and co-founder of Sugar King, emphasises the fundamentals of business planning – maintaining doomsday, middle-of-the-road and success scenarios; pivoting as needed; and knowing the product and clientele.
With costs, breaking even and engagement key to a concept’s survival, it’s easy to assume that bars must consistently predict what the drinking public will enjoy. While many recent closures were speakeasies opened in the 2010s, this year also saw an influx of neighbourhood bars. Bar Leone took home World’s Best Bar, as other “community first” concepts spread their wings: Mius, Oasis and Sugar King, to name a few, focus on simple drinks and convivial atmospheres to great success.

Nugent feels that predicting tastes is only part of the equation, however. “I think it’s a dangerous question to read into drinking preferences,” he says. “We want to make sure that every guest who walks into our venue is treated like they’re getting away from the doldrums of everyday life. Whether you’re serving a fast food item, a curated cocktail or a fine dining meal, you’re giving the best hospitality you can.
“We have to ask ourselves, ‘Is it the guest’s job to keep these places afloat or is the onus on us?’ How do we keep creating new and exciting ways for guests to come back? To take on these challenges, you really have to love the industry we’re in – otherwise, there’s no reason to get out of bed to take on these challenges.”

For Cheung, this focus on the guest makes training another key challenge. “In 2010, we needed people who could master technique,” she says. “Today, that’s the baseline. Now, we need storytellers, hosts, diplomats. They need to navigate social media, understand global trends, manage guest allergies and preferences with ease, and be ambassadors for a vast portfolio of spirits. The skill is in connection, making a guest feel uniquely seen in a 60-second interaction.”
“The challenge is sustaining interest past the two-year mark,” Cheung continues. “We need to consistently turn first-time visitors into regulars, and regulars into ambassadors. There’s no foot traffic to cushion the early months – just the steady, growing trust of people who make the effort to find you. It’s a marathon of consistent excellence, not a sprint.”

“Consistent quality in product and service leads to positive word of mouth and returning customers,” Lo notes. “The ‘hype’ window is like the honeymoon for a new establishment. It reflects the reality that initial excitement [can fade] unless sustained by quality and engagement.”
So is it due to these shifting sands, or a venue’s glaring inability to serve guests or offer something unique – to capture lightning in a half-to-two-year bottle – that causes it to close? Not necessarily. When 001’s original NoHo lease was ending, Cheung explains, the bar was not facing unmanageable financial pressures, but renewing would have locked the business into costs that Cheung and the team felt did not allow for growth or stability. The speakeasy, at its new location in Tai Kwun, celebrated its 15th anniversary last month.

Similarly, as the Post reported, Singular Concepts CEO David Sit attributed The Daily Tot’s closure to rent increases when it was attempting to renew its lease. “We hold onto the concept and the spirit of The Daily Tot, and hope to be back in the near future,” Sit stated in a press release. Reopening has come about for 001 and Dr Fern’s Gin Parlour – now The Doctor’s Residence at The Pottinger – though it is its own gamble.
“I’d say we were 80 per cent confident [we would reopen],” Cheung said. “We had the will, the brand, the team’s core and a strong reputation. But finding the right home – one that matched our ethos and made financial sense – was the 20 per cent gamble.” To reopen, a bar has to “protect its soul, not just the logo”, she advises. “Wait for the location that doesn’t just fit your budget, but fulfils your narrative. A rushed comeback can do more damage than a graceful exit.”

Some venues have indeed exited gracefully and given their mixologists room to develop something new. Nugent previously helmed the beverage programme at Lily & Bloom, a restaurant and bar in Lan Kwai Fong that closed in 2018. In 2020, he opened The Diplomat, a venue known as much for its bar bites and midnight cookies as its classics-based, martini-forward menu and one of the best Irish coffees in town. The opening of Sugar King over the summer has only added to the throngs of imbibers crowding Peel Street every weekend.
“The beauty, but also the curse of Hong Kong, is that it’s ever changing,” Nugent says. “These venues [that have closed] were incredible in their own right; they offered something that was part of the ethos of Hong Kong hospitality.
“We wish nothing but the best for anybody and everyone involved with these places. Who knows? One closure could [lead to] another opening of something even more incredible.”

Ultimately, whether it’s staving off closure, reopening or building something new, guest support and word of mouth go a long way towards keeping valued concepts alive. “One visit a month from a true regular is worth 10 one-time visits from trend chasers,” Cheung affirms. “Be a regular, come on a Tuesday, bring someone new. That’s the oxygen that lets a concept like ours live, breathe and evolve over 15 years.”

data.title: Culled bear meat sparks Japan restaurant feeding frenzy: ‘juicy, delicious’ and sold out
data.description:
Since Japan recorded a spike in deadly bear attacks, Koji Suzuki has struggled to keep up with booming demand for grilled cuts of the animal at his restaurant.
Cooked on a stone slate – or in a hot pot with vegetables – the meat comes from bears culled to curb maulings that have killed a record 13 people this year.
Suzuki’s eatery in the hilly city of Chichibu near Tokyo also serves deer and wild boar, but bear has surged in popularity after months of headlines about the animals breaking into homes, wandering near schools and rampaging through supermarkets.
“With news about bears growing, the number of customers who want to eat their meat has increased a lot,” Suzuki, 71, said.
As a show of respect for the bear’s life, “it’s better to use the meat at a restaurant like this, rather than burying it”, Suzuki, who is also a hunter, said.
His wife Chieko, 64, who runs the restaurant, said she now frequently turns away customers, but declined to say exactly how much business has grown.
One diner who grabbed a seat, 28-year-old composer Takaaki Kimura, was trying bear for the first time.
“It’s so juicy, and the more you chew, the tastier it gets,” he said, grinning as he and his friends sat around the grilling stone and bubbling pot.

By culling the bears, which can weigh up to half a tonne and outrun a human, officials hope to stem the threat across parts of northern Japan.
The 13 people killed in bear encounters this year doubles the previous record, with four months of the financial year still to go.
According to scientists, the crisis is being driven by a fast-growing bear population, combined with a falling human population and poor acorn harvest pushing bears to seek food elsewhere.
Scrambling to respond, the government has deployed troops to provide logistical help for trapping and hunting the animals.
Riot police have also been tasked with shooting them and the total culled in the first half of this financial year has surpassed the 9,100 killed across the whole of 2023-24.
Although far from an everyday dish, bear has long been eaten in mountainous villages across Japan.
The government hopes the meat can become a source of income for rural communities.
“It is important to turn the nuisance wildlife into something positive,” the farm ministry said earlier this month.


Local authorities will receive US$118 million in subsidies to control bear populations and promote sustainable consumption.
Some restaurants need no convincing.
Katsuhiko Kakuta, 50, who runs a village-owned restaurant in Aomori, one of the regions hardest hit by bear attacks, said he sold out of the meat earlier this month.
“It has been popular since we started serving it in 2021, but this year, our facility has got a lot of attention, especially after an influencer posted about us,” he said.
In a dimly lit French restaurant in Sapporo, the biggest city on the main northern island of Hokkaido, chef Kiyoshi Fujimoto sears rolled up meat from a brown bear before popping it into a pot of red wine sauce.
“I feel it’s good to use a locally sourced ingredient,” he said from the chic fine-dining spot, where a multicourse meal, including a consomme made from bear, costs around US$70.
“I think there are more people wanting to eat it now than before, and I’ve been stocking up to capitalise on this,” he said.
“Most people who eat it say it’s delicious.”

Brown bears are found only in Hokkaido, where their population has doubled over three decades to more than 11,500 as of 2023. Japanese black bears, meanwhile, are common across large parts of the country.
Last year, the government added bears to the list of animals subject to population control, reversing protection that had helped the mammals thrive.
Hokkaido plans to cull 1,200 bears annually over the next decade.
Much of the bear meat, however, still goes to waste, partly due to a shortage of government-approved processing facilities.
Japan has 826 game factories nationwide, but only a handful in northern prefectures hit hardest by attacks.
Kakuta’s restaurant has its own butchery, supplying bear meat dishes to a nearby hotel.
“Bear meat is a tourism resource for us,” he said. “And we use something that would otherwise be buried as garbage.”

data.title: Do Singapore’s athletes need more funding? Official’s ‘come to party’ review spurs debate
data.description:
A statement by the secretary general of the Singapore National Olympic Council (SNOC) that athletics had to “come to the party” for the city state to attain better SEA Games results has elicited a heated response from the local athletics community and others in the sporting fraternity.
The saga also sparked discussion on whether Singapore’s athletes were receiving sufficient monetary rewards for achievements and rigorous development pathways.
SNOC chief Mark Chay made the comments on Saturday during a review of Singapore’s performance in the 33rd edition of the Games, which were held in Bangkok from December 9 to Saturday.
Chay applauded the efforts of the country’s 926-strong sports contingent, but said “we do need to inculcate this competitive edge”. He added: “If not, what we’re doing is that we’re encouraging satisfactory performances, where our athletes are satisfied with not winning.”
Singapore finished fifth in the medal standings at the Games, with 202 medals – 52 golds, 61 silvers and 89 bronzes. Hosts Thailand topped the table with 500 medals – 233 golds, 154 silvers and 113 bronzes.
Thirty-nine athletes represented Singapore in athletics events, closing the Games with nine medals – three golds, three silvers and three bronzes.

In comparison, the country’s swimmers won 34 medals, including 19 golds, while its fencers went to the podium 13 times, including eight golds.
Chay, a former national swimmer, compared Singapore’s performance in swimming and athletics, noting that swimming garnered “50 per cent of the gold medals”. In contrast, the country’s athletics team won three gold medals out of 47 events.
He said athletics had to “come to the party” for Singapore to finish among the top three nations and pull off a strong showing in 2029, the year the city state is set to host the Games.
In response, Lien Choong Luen, president of the national sporting association Singapore Athletics, shared on social media the challenges faced by local athletes in the sport.
Lien hinted at greater competition unique to athletics, citing lower barriers to entry and a larger population in neighbouring countries.

Athletics was also made up of different sub-sports, such as running, jumping and throwing. “Each of these specialities requires a separate ecosystem of coaches, equipment and training – so the top of the funnel is much larger than in comparable sports, and requires proportionately more investment,” he said.
The association had engaged in aggressive fundraising activities over the past few years, but a long-term financing pipeline was needed to give athletes certainty, Lien said.
Miranda Teo, the association’s vice-president, also took to social media and pointed out that 80 per cent of athletics athletes were funded through resources raised by the association, underscoring the need for stronger systemic support.
Marathoner Soh Rui Yong chimed in, saying that Singapore was “sending a bunch of full-time students, software engineers, teachers, government employees, and corporate workers to compete against full-time athletes who were sponsored by organisations and private companies.”
He added: “Singapore has the resources to fund a proper high-performance athletics programme if it wants to, but those resources don’t seem to be flowing into the sport. Till then, don’t expect to win many gold medals bringing knives to a gun fight.”
Soh won two golds in the marathon event from previous editions of the Games, and a silver in the 10,000m event. He finished the 10,000m event in Bangkok in seventh place.

The debate also focused on whether Singapore was adequately rewarding athletes for their sporting achievements, with many calling for increased monetary compensation.
Soh on Tuesday reposted a graphic shared by Cali Amaral, Indonesia’s triathlon coach, which detailed how much Southeast Asian nations paid athletes for winning a gold at the 2025 Games.
Indonesian athletes were rewarded the most for topping the podium at US$59,595, and Thai athletes were given a 300,000 baht (US$9,620) bonus for the same achievement.
Under SNOC’s programme for major games awards, a gold win for an individual, a team event and a team sport would get a payout of S$10,000 (US$7,750), S$15,000 and S$30,000, respectively. There are no bonuses for silver and bronze medals.
Soh noted that despite having the highest cost of living in the region, Singapore was far from the top regarding gold medal payouts. He also revealed that there were no bonuses for silver and bronze medallists.
“An Indonesian winning gold is set for three years, with the cost of living in Indonesia. A Singaporean winning gold? It might last you three months if you’re frugal,” he wrote on social media.

Other athletes have urged the government to provide better support and carve out easier pathways for the sporting fraternity in their pursuits, and for the private sector to boost funding and investment.
National kayaker Brandon Ooi said in a comment on Soh’s post that instead of focusing on prize money, Singapore should work to change the perception that taking up sport was a “sacrifice” that came at the expense of better prospects elsewhere.
He also noted that Malaysia and Vietnam offered fewer rewards for medals, but still defeated Singapore in the 2025 Games tally. Malaysia offers national athletes 20,000 ringgit (US$4,920) for their first SEA Games gold medal and 10,000 ringgit for subsequent golds won.
Ooi, who bagged two silvers in Thailand, said: “I see this as a service to the nation, and so a way we can make athletes civil servants, or do something that reduces the opportunity cost of choosing this route, could help way more than prize money.”
One social media user said: “Discovering sporting talent is already hard enough, yet it is exponentially harder to persevere and sacrifice so much in terms of life advancement to be a Singapore athlete.
“I hope more can be done to make the journey fairer to future athletes.”

data.title: Teen ‘mastermind’ among 20 held by Hong Kong police over HK$2 million phone fraud
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested 20 people over a series of fraud cases in which a 19-year-old college student allegedly led a gang posing as mainland secret agents to swindle victims out of more than HK$2 million (US$257,000).
Cheung Wai-shan, chief inspector of the force’s regional crime unit in Western district, said on Wednesday that the group – 16 men and four women aged between 11 and 84 – were arrested during a two-day operation earlier this week for various offences, including money laundering, fraud and wasting police resources.
Among the crimes were five impersonation scams believed to have been led by the 19-year-old.
“Police received reports from victims who received calls from scammers claiming to be secret mainland agents between June and December this year,” Cheung said.
“The victims were accused of being involved in mainland economic crimes and were instructed to hand over funds to the suspect claiming to be a special agent. Five victims paid a total of over HK$1.5 million in cash.”
Cheung said the 19-year-old mastermind was arrested in Tsz Wan Shan on Tuesday. He had initially admitted to the offences and was being detained for further investigation, she added.

Police investigations found that the suspect had met victims in person and used a string of numbers as a code for identification in all five cases.
The suspect also showed the victims alleged confidential orders purportedly issued by mainland public security authorities, bearing their names to enhance credibility.
Among the evidence seized from the alleged mastermind’s home were the purported confidential orders and mobile phones used in the crimes.
Cheung added that police would not rule out pressing further charges against the suspect, who was also believed to be linked to cases in Sha Tin and Sai Kung districts.
“Mainland and overseas law enforcement officers absolutely cannot enforce laws within Hong Kong territory, nor will they ever ask citizens to submit any security deposits,” said Wong Wai-ping, senior inspector of the Western district.
She said police had also noticed that some fraud syndicates were using promises of quick money and high salaries to recruit young people as their pawns – for example, by asking them to deliver so-called confidential agreements or collect security deposits.
“Police emphasise that participating in these types of activities is also against the law,” she said.
According to the Theft Ordinance, obtaining property by deception is a serious offence that carries a maximum penalty of 10 years’ imprisonment.

data.title: 7 scientists, mathematicians, physicists and experts who moved to China in 2025
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest stories about scientists in China this year. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. After decades in the US, star Chinese mathematician couple returns home
Mathematician Chen Min has left Purdue University’s maths department to join the Eastern Institute of Technology, Ningbo as a full-time faculty member, the latest Chinese academic to return home from the US.
2. Maths star Joshua Zahl leaves Canada for China after solving century-old puzzle
China has secured a major academic coup with the recruitment of mathematics luminary Joshua Zahl, recently celebrated for solving the more than 100-year-old three-dimensional Kakeya conjecture.
3. Mathematician Zhong Xiao leaves Finland for China with award-winning work
Award-winning mathematician Professor Zhong Xiao has departed Finland after nearly three decades, bringing his foundational research critical to artificial intelligence back to China.
4. Why did Harvard top mathematician Liu Jun leave the US for China?

The professor’s decision to return to Tsinghua University amid rising tensions and US funding cuts had “nothing to do with Trump”.
5. Top engineer involved in Boeing 787 and A380 design leaves US for China
Zhou Ming, renowned as the mastermind behind key industrial software used in planes such as the Boeing 787 and Airbus A380, has left his leadership role at US-based global engineering giant Altair to return to China.
6. Prominent computer scientist Chen Jing leaves US to teach in China
Prominent blockchain researcher Chen Jing has left the United States to join Tsinghua University in Beijing as a full-time professor.
7. Biomathematician Hu Yijuan is among Chinese scientists in continuing US exodus

Renowned biomathematician Hu Yijuan has returned to China and joined the prestigious Peking University – her alma mater – after nearly two decades in the United States.

data.title: Craving art, music and theatre this Christmas? Here’s what to see in Hong Kong
data.description:
The festive season is upon Hong Kong, and as in past years, the city offers an array of cultural events for residents and visitors – from ballet to theatre and music.
While unmistakably Christmas-themed events are available, some offer an alternative for those who want to spend their holidays in a slightly different way.
The Post highlights some of the shows worth catching over the holidays.
Family-friendly performances
The Hong Kong Ballet’s annual performances of The Nutcracker mark the fifth anniversary of the city-inspired version created by the group’s artistic director, Septime Webre, featuring local elements, from dim sum to racing jockeys.
The shows, staged at the Hong Kong Cultural Centre in Tsim Sha Tsui, opened on December 12 and run through Boxing Day, with tickets priced from HK$180 to HK$1,200 (US$23 to US$154).
For those craving a touch of snow – the cinematic kind, at least – Frozen in Concert with the Hong Kong Philharmonic Orchestra offers a family-friendly escape into a wintry fantasy, bringing all the beloved characters from the Disney animation to life.
Tickets for the four concerts at the Hong Kong Cultural Centre in Tsim Sha Tsui, held from December 18 to 20, cost between HK$260 and HK$680 and feature crowd-pleasing numbers such as Do You Want to Build a Snowman? and Let It Go.
Looking for an art fix?
The West Kowloon Cultural District’s M+ museum has unveiled Asia’s first retrospective on Chinese-French artist Zao Wou-Ki’s printmaking, featuring nearly 180 exhibits that offer a rare glimpse into the abstract master’s lesser-known creations.
Titled “Zao Wou-Ki: Master Printmaker”, the show highlights the artist’s signature fusion of Eastern and Western aesthetics through lithographs donated by his wife and daughter, revealing a more intimate side of his artistic practice.
“This exhibition is a tribute to my husband [Zao’s] lifelong dedication to art. Zao considered printmaking as a realm of inexhaustible possibility,” said Françoise Marquet-Zao, president of the Zao Wou-Ki Foundation.
Tickets that also grant access to other parts of the museum are priced at HK$190 for adults and HK$100 for concessionary entry. The exhibition will run until May 3 next year.

A stone’s throw away from M+ is the Palace Museum, where one could experience the grandeur of ancient Egypt with 250 artefacts, including mummies, jewellery and a striking statue of Tutankhamun, offering a peek into a civilisation more than 3,000 years old.
Visitors can also try their luck at the museum’s gift shop for souvenirs from the exhibition “Ancient Egypt Unveiled: Treasures from Egyptian Museums”, some of which have already sold out since its opening last month.
The exhibition runs until August 31, 2026, with tickets priced at HK$190 for adults and HK$95 for concessions. Other discounts and ticket combinations are also available.

What else?
Over on Hong Kong Island, the Tang Shu-wing Theatre Studio is staging a Cantonese adaptation of the French tragicomedy Waiting for Godot by Nobel Prize-winning Irish playwright Samuel Beckett, running until December 28 at the Hong Kong Arts Centre.
The production features acclaimed actors, including Hong Kong Film Award “Best Actor” winner Lam Ka-tung, in the iconic play that has seen numerous global iterations, including past versions starring Ian McKellen and Patrick Stewart.

data.title: Hong Kong opens tender for next Kai Tak Cruise Terminal operator with stricter KPIs
data.description:
The next operator of Hong Kong’s Kai Tak Cruise Terminal will manage the entire site, including public areas, and face financial penalties if it underperforms, according to a new government tender aimed at transforming the area into an attractive tourist destination.
Authorities on Wednesday launched an open tender for the next operator of the terminal in Kowloon, with the new term set to begin on June 1, 2028. The winning bidder will manage the facility for 10 years, with a possible five-year extension based on performance.
“The smooth operation of the [Kai Tak Cruise Terminal] is vital to attracting more cruise ships to berth in Hong Kong and promoting the development of the industry in the city,” a spokesman for the Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau said.
“The government is also committed to elevating the function of the [terminal] as a cruise terminal for all and making good use of the terminal facilities.”
Although the current 10-year tenancy held by Worldwide Cruise Terminals will not expire until May 2028, authorities previously said they would advance the tendering process to allow a longer handover period and address the “software” failures identified in recent years.
Officials earlier said the new contract would set out stricter performance indicators and broader responsibilities for the operator, expanding its role beyond basic berthing operations to include more proactive commercial development and destination marketing.
The tender notice said that, in addition to managing cruise operations, the next operator would also be responsible for the Podium Gardens on the second floor, the terminal’s rooftop park and other ancillary commercial areas.
The goal is to allow private or non-profit organisations to rent such spaces for commercial, cultural, or sporting events, especially during the cruise low season, so the site can be better utilised year-round.

The operator would also be subject to key performance indicators designed to measure proactiveness, such as the annual number of ship calls, non-cruise events held, footfall generated by those events and the occupancy rate of retail and ancillary areas.
But financial penalties for failing to meet these targets would only take effect from the third year of the tenancy.
Interested parties would be assessed on two major categories – 70 per cent based on non-pricing proposals such as experience, execution plans and KPI targets, and the remaining 30 per cent on their price proposals.
The terminal, which opened in 2013 at a final cost of around HK$8.2 billion (US$1.05 billion), has long been criticised for poor transport connectivity and underused commercial spaces.
Although the government originally designed the site to anchor Hong Kong as a regional cruise hub, critics and lawmakers often described it as a “white elephant” because of its isolated location on the former airport runway.
The frustrations peaked in August 2023, when Royal Caribbean’s Spectrum of the Seas became the first major post-pandemic cruise to dock in the city as Hong Kong’s struggling tourism sector sought to recover.
But arriving tourists complained of long waits for taxis and other public transport, causing major embarrassment for the government, which was forced to introduce emergency transport measures and launch a high-level review of the terminal’s management.
Tourism chief Rosanna Law Shuk-pui said the decision to launch the tender now was necessary to give the new operator, or the incumbent if it chooses to re-bid, enough time to overhaul the facility’s operations.
The move also aligns with the government’s updated “Development Blueprint for Hong Kong’s Tourism Industry,” which seeks to integrate the terminal more closely with the surrounding Kai Tak development, including the newly opened Kai Tak Sports Park.

data.title: 2025 in timepieces: 9 top moments in watches this year, from Donald Trump’s tariffs to the return of Urban Jürgensen
data.description:
It’s been another year of twists and turns, highs and lows for the world of horology. LVMH Watch Week and Watches and Wonders kicked off the horological year in style as usual, but fairs like Geneva Watch Days and Dubai Watch Week in the latter half of the year grew significantly larger as well. US President Donald Trump’s tariffs dominated headlines for the global watch industry this year, generally affecting operations and price adjustments as tariffs were paused, resumed, increased and decreased.
Still, brands found time to make big moves. They signed new ambassadors and were seen on celebrity wrists not just on red carpets but at births, engagements and movie promotions, just to name a few. Urban Jürgensen got relaunched with Kari Voutilainen leading the charge, while in Hong Kong, local collectors created stunning new pieces via all-new brands to give enthusiasts even more options.
Here are 9 of the major timepiece moments that marked 2025.
LVMH Watch Week pushed ahead

LVMH Watch Week almost became a victim of the Los Angeles wildfires, but after the conglomerate relocated the event to New York and Paris, brands showed up ready to put their best wrists forward.

Bulgari, Hublot, Tag Heuer, Tiffany & Co. and Zenith played the hits as the likes of the Serpenti, Big Bang, Carrera and Chronomaster Sport appeared in stunning new iterations. The Serpenti’s Tubogas and Seduttori lines received their own self-winding movements in the form of the BVS 100 Lady Solotempo calibre, a first for the brand, while Zenith’s Chronomaster Sport received rainbow gem-setting treatment. Tiffany & Co. leaned heavily on its legendary Tiffany diamond to give us two stunning Carat 128 Facet references, in maximalist yellow gold and the brand’s signature aquamarine.

There were no rules when it came to looks. Gérald Genta’s Gentissima Oursin was set with 137 fire opals, resulting in a show-stopping orange silhouette. Daniel Roth veered in the other direction, unveiling a discreet, time-only Extra Plat Souscription in a shift away from the brand’s successful and more familiar tourbillon models.

As always, Louis Vuitton gave us new Tambours few could have expected, with the Taiko Spin Time showing digital hours; and the Convergence, which is dominated by a digital display at 12 o’clock and, strikingly, not much else, in a glowing display of minimalism.
Major trends at Watches and Wonders

The biggest event on the high horology calendar landed at Palexpo in Geneva during the first week of April, and the Style team was on the ground to take a closer look at all the new releases. Sixty brands showcased their novelties in stunning booths to over 55,000 attendees. This year’s edition was also arguably one of the most star-studded yet, as Kim Woo-bin visited Jaeger-LeCoultre, Jay Chou visited Tudor, Eileen Gu and Simone Ashley visited IWC Schaffhausen, Gianna Jun visited Piaget, and Kylian Mbappé visited Hublot along with Usain Bolt, just to name a few.

Brands had a lot to say this year. Rolex officially unveiled its first five-hertz movement with the Land-Dweller, while Vacheron Constantin celebrated its 270th anniversary and also released the most complicated wristwatch ever made (the Les Cabinotiers Solaria). Bulgari made its first-ever appearance at the fair, debuting the thinnest tourbillon watch in the world, while Cartier revisited its Tank à Guichets, an art deco jumping hours watch design from the 1920s.
We covered the fair in-depth – most notably the major trends present at this year’s event, and why gold was so prominent.

High horology is already looking at 2026 as Watches and Wonders announces a few of the new brands joining the line-up for the world’s biggest watch fair. Next April, Audemars Piguet rejoins the line-up after departing in 2019, when Watches and Wonders was still called the Salon International de la Haute Horlogerie. Heavyweights such as Corum and Grand Seiko sister brand Credor are also joining, along with rising sports watchmaker Bianchet and dependable pilot watch favourite Sinn Spezialuhren. L’Epée 1839 represents the art of clockmaking – LVMH bought the clockmaker last July, the month after the conglomerate joined the Watches and Wonders board.
Watches were affected by Trump’s tariffs

While Watches and Wonders was abuzz over new releases, by day two, excited conversations had turned to anxious speculation as US President Donald Trump announced tariff increases on major trade partners earlier this April. Most notably for watches, Trump announced reciprocal tariff increases on some of the world’s leading watch-producing countries, affecting retailers, brands and collectors alike. He imposed a 31 per cent tariff on goods from Switzerland, 24 per cent on Japan and 20 per cent on Germany (as part of the EU).

This had a number of ramifications for watch consumers in the US, which – according to the Federation of the Swiss Watch Industry’s 2024 report – was the world’s highest spending country on watches in 2024, 2023 and 2022. Collectors around the world may need to pay exorbitant prices for new watches made in any of the aforementioned countries – even after Trump announced a 90-day pause, levying a 10 per cent flat increase in the meantime.
Price increases are slowly being rolled out, or at least heavily teased, by brands across the board – even in an already weakening watch market. Forbes recently reported that exports from major watch conglomerates had decreased in the last year.

Hodinkee reported in April that while the tariffs may not affect bigger brands with substantial stock already in the US, mid-tier, direct-to-consumer brands such as Christopher Ward have no recourse except to pass on the cost to US clients, and CEO Mike France says he already expects demand to slip. The platform later reported that the US and Switzerland had agreed to lower tariffs to 15 per cent from 39 per cent, as jobs in the Swiss watch industry declined on an annual basis for the first time since 2021.
Watch ambassadors

We saw a handful of brands truly raise the bar for what it means to put high horology on celebrity wrists. For instance, Omega’s July announcement of Aaron Taylor-Johnson as a brand ambassador was buoyed with rumours that the actor may well be the next James Bond, given the spy franchise’s storied history with the Seamaster 300M.
Urban Jürgensen makes a powerful comeback

June marked the grand return of Danish independent watchmaker Urban Jürgensen. The brand was bought out in 2021 by a cohort of investors led by now co-CEO and watchmaking legend Kari Voutilainen, co-CEO Alex Rosenfield, and silent co-owner Andrew Rosenfield.

Urban Jürgensen has been in operation since 1773, founded by Jürgen Jürgensen and taken over by his son, Urban, in 1811. The brand became known for producing marine chronometers for the Danish navy, and has been heavily associated with high horology since, particularly through the 1980s to 2000s under Peter Baumberger – a period in which Voutilainen also first became associated with the brand.

In Santa Monica in June, the brand celebrated its revival in style, with actresses Rashida Jones and Leighton Meester, filmmaker David Katzenberg, and even Kyle Kuzma of the Milwaukee Bucks in attendance. The brand announced three new timepieces to mark the occasion: the UJ-1 250th Anniversary Watch, the UJ-2 and the UJ-3.
The first celebrates the Oval Pocket Watch and adds a deadbeat seconds complication, while the second features a double wheel natural escapement inspired by Abraham-Louis Breguet, who mentored the young Urban Jürgensen. The final piece is a collaboration between Voutilainen and watchmaker/engineer Andreas Strehler, which combines a moonphase accurate to 14,000 years, a perpetual calendar and a double wheel natural escapement. The UJ-2 took home the Men’s Watch Prize at the Grand Prix D’Horlogerie de Genève in November.
Taylor Swift sported Cartier while announcing her engagement, and other major celeb watch moments

The year was so rife with celebrities wearing big watches for big moments, we were hard pressed to pick just a few to recap here.
“When your gym and English teachers are finally engaged,” as Taylor Swift wrote in her Instagram caption about the occasion, we at Style immediately wondered what type of wrist candy she would wear to complement her new engagement ring in August. She didn’t go with her usual gold Cartier Panthère, but with the discontinued Cartier Santos Demoiselle, according to Hodinkee – a timeless, gold and diamond piece to set the tone for the beginning of the rest of her and Kansas City Chiefs tight end Travis Kelce’s lives.

That same month, Rihanna gave birth to daughter Rocki Irish Mayers on September 13 – her third child with A$AP Rocky. She marked the occasion wearing a beautiful Audemars Piguet Royal Oak in rose gold with a pink dial, matching the “it’s a girl” pink of her photo shoot.
In October, Audemars Piguet creative conduit John Mayer was spotted sporting a custom Royal Oak that, at the time of his late September concert in Ann Arbor, Michigan, was completely covered in a design featuring just watch components. According to Time+Tide, the piece was later revealed to be a “road test” for the Audemars Piguet Royal Oak Extra-Thin Selfwinding Flying Tourbillon Chronograph, or RD#5. It houses the new calibre 8100, which uses a rack-and-pinion system to smooth the reset motion while saving energy.

That same month, Timothée Chalamet, known mainly for sporting Cartier, was seen with the comparatively larger Urban Jürgensen UJ-2 on an orange strap while promoting his new film, Marty Supreme.
Hong Kong’s home-grown watchmakers

This year, Hong Kong watchmakers and collectors took matters into their own hands with watches made in the city.
Paul Li founded Metrical Watch in 2024 and announced the Epiphany Origin earlier this year as the brand’s inaugural watch. The piece – similar to this year’s Breguet Classique Souscription – uses a single hand with which to track the minutes, while hour indication is merged with a day-night wheel, therefore needing only half the watch dial to reveal the time in an evocative way rarely seen in local watches.

James Wong and Tony Yip launched Nectere Watches with a boldly asymmetrical debut piece in the form of Order From Chaos. Inspired by the mathematical concept of chaos theory, the watch was fully funded via Kickstarter at over 10 times the original funding goal set in April, with the first orders fulfilled in October.

Meanwhile, Johnathan Chan and Stanley Ho turned to the slopes for Earthen Company’s first watch. The Summit was built to be an accessible ceramic piece, with a highly legible field watch design dialled in to the needs of skiers and other snowlovers. The collection’s alpine inspirations come in three colourways: a black case with black dial for the Overcast, a white case with black dial for the Whiteout, and a fully-lumed white case and dial for the Blue Bird.

Hong Kong watch club Watch Ho & Co celebrated two years of horological camaraderie by collaborating with Selten to produce Jui, meaning to gather. The mother-of-pearl patterned dial delivers subtle nods to the club’s local roots, such as a Chinese number 2 at the two o’clock position to celebrate the club’s years in existence.

Most recently, Mark Cho and Elliot Hammer – responsible for The Armoury’s watch collaborations with brands like Unimatic, Paulin and Ressence – launched the Series A debut collection for Temporal Works. According to a release, watches for Temporal Works are designed from a blank slate and prioritise clarity, wearability and timeless style. The 37mm Series A is powered by a Sellita SW210 movement, housed by a Zaratsu-polished case, a type of metal finishing usually seen on Grand Seiko pieces that creates distortion-free surfaces.
Watch fairs grow in scale

This year has been huge for watch fairs across the board in cities like Geneva, New York and Dubai – as we had mentioned, even Watches and Wonders is adding ever more brands to its April fair.
Geneva Watch Days in September added 14 brands to its roster, and New York became the biggest watch destination in October with Windup Watch Fair (presented by Worn & Wound), Rolliefest and the Fondation Haute Horlogerie. Most recently, Dubai Watch Week – organised by retailer Ahmed Seddiqi – recorded its largest edition yet boasting 49,000 visitors and over 90 exhibiting brands, many of whom released Dubai Watch Week-exclusive novelties.
GPHG 2025: And the winner is …

The 2025 edition of the Watch Oscars came to a resounding close as the winners of this year’s edition of the Grand Prix d’Horlogerie de Genève (GPHG) were announced in Geneva last month. Nineteen watches, brands or industry icons, selected by a jury of GPHG Academy members led by GPHG president Nick Foulkes, took home a prize out of a total of 90 nominated timepieces.

Breguet took home the Aiguille d’Or – or watch of the year – for its one-handed, minimalist Classique Souscription 2025, while Greubel Forsey’s hyper-complicated Nano Foudroyante took home the Mechanical Exception Watch Prize. Chopard took home the most awards for one brand, winning the Sports Watch Prize for its Alpine Eagle 41 SL Cadence 8HF and the Ladies’ Complication Prize for its Imperiale Four Seasons.

data.title: Shenhua absorbs parent’s coal assets as it looks to the future in a decarbonising China
data.description:
China Shenhua Energy’s 133 billion yuan (US$18.9 billion) acquisition of a dozen assets could help the mainland’s largest listed coal miner take on competition as the era of coal power starts winding down in the world’s largest market for the fossil fuel, according to analysts.
The miner in a filing to the Hong Kong stock exchange last week provided an update to its August proposal to acquire equity interests in 12 enterprises from its controlling shareholder, China Energy Investment Group.
The deal, which involves assets in coal production, power generation, coal chemical industry and coal logistics, is expected to take Shenhua’s total assets to over 200 billion yuan and expand its coverage to mines in the Xinjiang and Inner Mongolia autonomous regions.
“The deal reflects a broad move to reduce excessive competition and enhance efficiency among state-owned enterprises in China amid low profit margins and deflationary pressures,” said Gary Ng Cheuk-yan, a senior economist at Natixis Corporate and Investment Bank.


The acquisitions were aimed at realising the promise of ending the two-decade same-industry competition between Shenhua and its controlling shareholder, concentrating coal-focused assets into the listed company, according to Guosheng Securities.
In the last few years, Shenhua and its controlling shareholder signed several agreements to address the competition.
China Energy Investment Group produced 620 million tonnes of coal last year, far higher than Shenhua’s 327 million tonnes. However, the acquisitions are expected to expand Shenhua’s coal reserves by 65 per cent to 68.5 billion tonnes, production capacity by 57 per cent to 512 million tonnes a year and power generation capacity by 28 per cent. The assets will be injected into Shenhua by August 2028.
However, China’s coal production continues to decelerate due to the slowing economic growth and Beijing’s decarbonisation push. China’s coal production growth dropped to 1.3 per cent in 2024 from 9 per cent in 2022, implying production may be nearing a peak, according to Western Securities.
In addition to the oversupply, the International Energy Agency also projected in a report this month that China’s coal imports would continue to fall until 2030.
Lucas Zhang Liutong, director of Hong Kong-based consultancy WaterRock Energy Economics, said the consolidation would not help lift subdued coal prices, but it was instead meant to prepare for retiring coal power after 2030 as outlined by Beijing.
“All state-owned companies, including Shenhua, will need to think of gradually retiring their old coal capacity,” Zhang said.
Under Beijing’s goal to reach peak carbon emissions by 2030 and net zero by 2060, China had transformed over 360 gigawatt-hours of coal power capacity by the third quarter of 2024, exceeding the 200GWh target set for the end of 2025. The transformation was achieved mainly by reducing the proportion of coal with alternatives like biomass and green ammonia.
The Beijing-based think tank China Electricity Council projected in May that the share of coal power would decline to one-third of total power generation capacity by the end of this year amid a surge in renewable energy.
This also comes as Beijing’s three-year campaign to deepen reforms and transform state-owned enterprises (SOEs) ends this year.
In an interview last Monday, Zhang Yuzhuo, chairman of the State-owned Assets Supervision and Administration Commission, said the SOE reform over the next five years would focus on functions including strategic security, industrial leadership, national economic development and people’s livelihoods.
Shenhua said the restructuring was meant to accelerate the transformation and upgrade of the coal industry under the global energy transition and China’s decarbonisation goals.
The growth of the renewable power industry along with rising costs and hazards due to the increasing depths of mines has brought operational difficulties to the fore, said Fu Jianhua, president of the China National Coal Association, in a meeting on December 10, where representatives from at least 15 companies took part.

data.title: Lard can actually keep you fit and feeling more comfortable, Chinese study finds
data.description:
Consuming a diet with a moderate amount of lard can decrease both fat accumulation and inflammation in body fat, according to a new study by Chinese scientists.
“These findings challenge the conventional view of animal fats and demonstrate that consuming lard at the recommended level (with dietary fat providing 25 per cent of energy) can confer metabolic benefits by regulating adipose tissue homeostasis,” the team wrote.
Scientists from Hunan Agricultural University, the University of South China and the Changsha Agriculture and Rural Affairs Bureau published their findings in the peer-reviewed journal npj Science of Food this month.
In China, health researchers are facing a paradox with health and fat intake: obesity rates in the country have increased even though long-standing advice promotes the consumption of unsaturated fatty acids, such as those found in vegetable oils, over saturated fatty acids from animal fats like lard.
Chinese national dietary guidelines recommend 25 to 30 grams of cooking oil per day for adults. It suggests limiting fatty meats and animal organs consumption because they are high in saturated fat and cholesterol.
The team also pointed to previous studies on diets based on pork and lard which indicated that people who consume them show a “significantly lower obesity and diabetes prevalence” than those who follow plant-based or vegetarian diets.
“This paradox highlights the imperative for a more comprehensive understanding of the metabolic impacts of specific dietary fats and necessitates a critical re-evaluation of prevailing nutritional guidelines.”
In the new study, the team compared the effects of lard, camellia seed oil and peanut oil on body fat deposition. They fed mice with a whole food diet where fat provided 25 per cent of the animals’ energy intake for 24 weeks.


They chose whole food diets for the study where “fat is ingested as part of the entire food matrix rather than an isolated purified component” to minimise the disconnection between the experiment and the real world.
They found in mice that, compared with vegetable oils, a moderate intake of lard significantly reduced lipid accumulation.
Their analysis also showed that moderate lard intake elevated the level of serum taurocholic acid, a bile acid that acts as a detergent to solubilise fats for absorption.
This increase enhanced the expression of genes related to the breakdown of fats and facilitated the transition of pro-inflammatory cells into anti-inflammatory cells, according to the study.
“Lard, a traditional animal fat in China, was found in our previous research to reduce obesity incidence when combined with soybean oil,” they wrote.
They added that lard is rich in both saturated fatty acids and monounsaturated fatty acids, “which may improve insulin sensitivity and help regulate blood glucose levels”.
Lard has a higher ratio of omega-3 healthy fats to omega-6 compared with camellia seed and peanut oil. Since excessive omega-6 intake exacerbates tissue inflammation, using lard might lead to less inflammation in the body under the same energy intake.
“This advantage is further supported by our prior findings: an appropriate mixture of lard and sunflower oil (high in omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acids) significantly reduced hepatic triglyceride accumulation, serum free fatty acid levels and adipose tissue hypertrophy – exhibiting better anti-lipid deposition effects than sunflower oil alone.


“This confirms that lard’s fatty acid profile, when used in a certain proportion, inhibits excessive lipid accumulation and alleviates lipid overload-induced inflammation,” they wrote.
In December 2024, scientists from the team called for more research into the relationship between lard and diabetes and the health benefits of appropriate lard intake in a commentary in the same food science journal.
“Taking the present results and published data into comprehensive consideration, if no effective intervention actions are taken, the incidence of diabetes will grow sharply in those countries and areas with lard-excluding dietary patterns,” they wrote.
“In countries characterised by rapid economic development, represented by China, keeping lard and pork out of kitchens may be a causal risk factor for the constantly increasing incidence of diabetes.”
However, a very high fat intake has been found to be harmful, including in a mice study published by researchers in Vietnam last August.
They found that a 66-day exposure to a high-fat diet, with 60 per cent calories from pork hard fat, increased body weight in mice, expanded white fat depots, disrupted glucose tolerance and led to abnormal levels of fats, cholesterol and triglycerides in the bloodstream.
“A long-term intake of high fat from lard had a detrimental effect on health and a potential induction of the changes in the expression levels of lipogenesis genes in mice,” they concluded.
